content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Ms. Sue Sarkis 
Sarkis Detective Agency 
P. O. Box 6608 
Glendale, CA 
91205 
Dear Ms. Sarkis: 
October 21, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-88-386 
This is in response to your letter dated October 4, 1988. 
In your letter you ask whether a non-profit corporation to 
which you belong will lose its tax exempt status by making a 
contribution to a statewide initiative measure (Proposition 
100) that is on the ballot for November 8, 1988. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission is responsible for 
the administration and enforcement of the Political Reform Act 
of 1974. 
(See Government Code sections 81000-91015.) 
As part 
of its responsibilities, the Commission ensures that candidates 
and committees appropriately report campaign contributions that 
they receive. 
The contribution described in your letter, depending on its 
amount, may be subject to the disclosure requirement of the 
Political Reform Act. 
However, the Commission has neither the 
jurisdiction nor expertise to provide an opinion on the tax 
effect of such a contribution. 
This is something that you will 
have to obtain from the Franchise Tax Board or your tax 
advisor, if you have not already done so. 
If you have questions regarding this letter, please contact 
me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED: SH: ld 
Very truly yours, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General. counS~l"i ' . 
4 
j;, I!/~'_ 
By· 'Scott Hallabriri 
Counsel, Legal Division 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 9S804~0807 • 
(916)322~S660 


October 4, 1988 
SARKIS DETECTIVE AGENCY 
P.O. BOX 6608 • GLENDALE, CALIFORNIA 91205 
(818) 242·2505 
The Fair Political Practices Committee 
428 J Street 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Attention: 
Legal Department 
Dear Sirs: 
I belong to a California mutual benefit non-profit corpor-
ation which is federally classified for tax exemption 
under Code 064. 
We are considering contributing a sum 
of money to Proposition 100 which is an initiative on 
the current ballot. 
I have spoken with the Attorney General's Office, the 
State Franchise Tax Board and the Internal Revenue Service 
and have been informed by all three that we may not make 
said contribution. 
On the other hand, I have been informed 
by my corporate counsel that the Fair Political Practices 
Committee would not consider that a political contribution 
and therefore we may make such contribution. 
Maya non-profit mutual benefit tax exempt corporation 
make a contribution to Proposition 100 without being in 
jeopardy of losing their tax exempt status? 
The sections 
of violation I am 
concerned with is Section 23701(e) of 
the Revenue and Taxation Code along with 501(c) (3) or 
501(c) (6) of the Internal Revenue Section Code. 
I would like to thank you in advance for your expeditious 
handling of this matter. 
Sincerely yours, 
SUE SARKIS 
SS:by 
STATE LICENSE ;""6564 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Sue Sarkis 
Sarkis Detective Agency 
P.O. Box 668 
Glendale, CA 
91205 
Dear Ms. Sarkis: 
october 11, 1988 
Re: 
88-386 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on October 7, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Scott Hallabrin, an attorney in 
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329) .) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel

sections.question: Maya non-profit mutual benefit tax exempt corporation make a contribution to Proposition 100 without being in jeopardy of losing their tax exempt status? The sections of violation I am concerned with is Section 23701(e) of the Revenue and Taxation Code along with 501(c)(3) or 501(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Section Code.
sections.conclusion: The Fair Political Practices Commission is responsible for the administration and enforcement of the Political Reform Act of 1974. As part of its responsibilities, the Commission ensures that candidates and committees appropriately report campaign contributions that they receive. The contribution described in your letter, depending on its amount, may be subject to the disclosure requirement of the Political Reform Act. However, the Commission has neither the jurisdiction nor expertise to provide an opinion on the tax effect of such a contribution. This is something that you will have to obtain from the Franchise Tax Board or your tax advisor, if you have not already done so.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a non-profit mutual benefit corporation classified as tax-exempt under IRC Code 064 may contribute funds to Proposition 100 (a statewide initiative measure) without losing its tax-exempt status under California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 23701(e) or federal IRC Sections 501(c)(3) or 501(c)(6).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide advice on the tax implications of the contribution, stating that while the contribution may be subject to Political Reform Act disclosure requirements depending on its amount, the Commission lacks jurisdiction and expertise to opine on tax consequences. The requestor should consult the Franchise Tax Board or a tax advisor for guidance on whether the contribution would jeopardize the non-profit's tax-exempt status.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes incoming request letter (October 4, 1988), acknowledgment letter (October 11, 1988), and formal FPPC response (October 21, 1988). The response is a declination of the substantive question on jurisdictional grounds, though it confirms potential disclosure requirements under the Political Reform Act. File No. 1-88-386.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Maya non-profit mutual benefit tax exempt corporation make a contribution to Proposition 100 without being in jeopardy of losing their tax exempt status? The sections of violation I am concerned with is Section 23701(e) of the Revenue and Taxation Code along with 501(c)(3) or 501(c)(6) of the Internal Revenue Section Code.

CONCLUSION: The Fair Political Practices Commission is responsible for the administration and enforcement of the Political Reform Act of 1974. As part of its responsibilities, the Commission ensures that candidates and committees appropriately report campaign contributions that they receive. The contribution described in your letter, depending on its amount, may be subject to the disclosure requirement of the Political Reform Act. However, the Commission has neither the jurisdiction nor expertise to provide an opinion on the tax effect of such a contribution. This is something that you will have to obtain from the Franchise Tax Board or your tax advisor, if you have not already done so.

embedding.summary: Ms. Sarkis requested FPPC advice on whether her non-profit mutual benefit corporation could contribute funds to Proposition 100 without losing its tax-exempt status. The FPPC responded that while the contribution may be subject to Political Reform Act disclosure requirements, the Commission lacks jurisdiction to advise on tax consequences and referred her to the Franchise Tax Board or a tax advisor.
